video_title: What remains scarce after AGI? – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
gist: Two AI economists (Imas of DeepMind/Chicago, Trammell of Epoch/Stanford) argue that whether labor share survives AGI hinges on (a) whether humans intrinsically value human-in-the-loop 'relational' goods and (b) whether demand for capital/compute keeps expanding via increasing variety rather than satiating. They contend negative-growth and 'messy middle' scenarios require improbable conditions, that capital ownership and indexing—not retraining—are the key levers for capturing AGI gains, and that whether AGI is monopolized (like social media) or commoditized (like electricity) decides where rents accrue.

themes.theme: What stays scarce after AGI — the relational sector

insights.claim: After broad automation, value accrues to whatever stays scarce, and humans are naturally scarce, so the durable scarce category is the 'relational sector' — services/goods where a human being in the loop is itself part of the product's value.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Investors hunting AGI-proof cash flows should look at businesses whose value depends on verified human involvement (live performance, in-person care, human-attested trust), not at commoditizable output.
insights.quote: What is scarce tells you where the value will accrue.

insights.claim: The relational thesis only holds if a human is 'not a horse' — i.e., humans must add value to the output itself (replacing them lowers output value), not merely be a substitutable input; if that intrinsic premium is weak or covers few sectors, labor share still collapses to zero.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: The bull case for human labor post-AGI rests on an empirical fact nobody has measured; treat 'humans stay valuable' theses as unproven optionality, not base case.
insights.quote: if you replace the human, the value of the output decreases

insights.claim: An incentive-compatible art-print experiment found human-made prints command a large premium over identical AI prints, and that premium collapses when supply rises from 1 to 500 units — while AI prints show no scarcity effect because AI output is already treated as a commodity.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Human premia are tied to perceived one-to-one connection and scarcity; scalable 'human-made' branding may not retain pricing power, while AI output is structurally a commodity with no scarcity rent.
insights.quote: AI is already viewed as a commodity.

insights.claim: The right reference class for relational jobs is not the ballerina but multi-task white-collar jobs (e.g., doctors) where most tasks are automatable except a human-delivered diagnosis/support task consumers will pay extra to keep human.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Screen service businesses by whether the residual human task carries willingness-to-pay; that residual, not the whole job, is the defensible margin.

insights.claim: We have essentially no data to identify which jobs are relational — there is no conjoint analysis of willingness-to-pay for keeping a human in the loop versus full automation — so all forecasts here are unanchored.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Whoever first collects credible relational/elasticity data gains real edge; current macro-AI forecasts are speculation, so size positions accordingly.
insights.quote: I've been saying we need a Manhattan Project for data.

themes.theme: Labor share, capital share, and the Kaldor constancy

insights.claim: For centuries roughly 60% of the economy has been paid to labor as wages and 30-40% to capital, and this constancy held through the entire Industrial Revolution — a Kaldor fact that is surprising and central to whether AGI changes the split.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Bets on a labor-share collapse are bets against centuries of empirical constancy; demand high evidence before pricing in a structural break.
insights.quote: For many hundreds of years, ~60% of the economy basically gets paid out to humans in wages

insights.claim: Claims that labor share has been falling for 20-30 years may be an accounting artifact — Atkinson's work shows that holding accounting methods constant, labor share hasn't fallen at all.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: The popular 'labor share is already declining' narrative may be measurement noise; don't extrapolate it into AGI scenarios.
insights.quote: if you keep the accounting constant over the years, labor share hasn't even fallen ever

insights.claim: On a network-adjusted basis (tracing the whole supply chain, not just the final step), nothing has yet been fully automated — US computer and electronic products have a stable network-adjusted capital share of only ~50%, because labor adds large value upstream.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Even highly 'automated' goods still embed ~50% labor up the chain; full capital-share=1 goods would be a genuine regime change worth watching for as a signal.
insights.quote: Computer and electronic products in the US have a very stable network-adjusted capital share of around 50%.

insights.claim: The coming qualitative shift is that some goods will have their network-adjusted capital share go to one (a fully automated supply chain with no point where a human is intrinsically wanted), but the net effect on aggregate capital share is ambiguous.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Don't assume full automation of a sector mechanically lifts economy-wide capital share; satiation in those goods can offset it.
insights.quote: there will be at least some goods whose network-adjusted capital share goes to one

insights.claim: If fully automated 'everything else' satiates fast, quantity goes to infinity but marginal utility falls faster, so spending shifts to scarce human goods — meaning aggregate capital share could fall, not rise, after full automation.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Satiation dynamics, not just automation, drive where income lands; a sector being automated does not guarantee its owners capture more of the pie.

themes.theme: Increasing variety vs. satiation — the master variable

insights.claim: Whether labor share survives hinges on increasing variety: if new varieties of capital goods keep appearing fast enough that consumers never satiate, income keeps flowing to capital; if there is no comparable variety growth in human goods, labor share goes to zero.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: The single most important question for AGI macro outcomes is the pace of new-variety creation in capital goods vs. satiation; track product-variety expansion as the leading indicator.

insights.claim: A 1400s Mongolian forecasting scarcity would have wrongly predicted satiation in 'horse-transport, yogurt and yurts' leaving all spending on singers — but variety expansion kept the singer share negligible; by analogy the speakers' central (uncertain) prediction is the human-intrinsic share stays small post-AGI.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: History favors variety expansion keeping human-only spending marginal; the base case is capital/automated goods dominate, with human-premium niches staying small.
insights.quote: the share spent on singers has stayed negligible

insights.claim: Chad Jones's result shows the share of the economy spent on transistors/computing has been decreasing even as transistor counts grew trillions-fold, because the marginal value of computation kept falling — 'every 18 months the value of computation halves.'
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Historically compute spend shrank as a share; the AGI bet is precisely that this finally reverses — a regime change to watch in compute's share of GDP.
insights.quote: every 18 months, the value of computation halves

insights.claim: For the first time the historic pattern may be breaking: an H100 costs more to rent now than three years ago despite superior, more abundant compute, because smarter models raise the opportunity cost of compute — a sign demand for compute may not satiate.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Rising GPU rental prices despite abundance is the concrete signal that compute demand is expanding faster than supply — bullish for compute owners and the case for rising capital share.
insights.quote: an H100 costs more to rent now than it did three years ago

insights.claim: Software is a good whose demand keeps rising as it gets cheaper, so the relevant question for compute is how many new uses are found per unit of compute — the 'ultimate question' for whether compute's share of the economy keeps climbing.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Monitor whether AI keeps unlocking new compute uses; sustained non-satiation is the structural bull thesis for compute and capital, satiation the bear thesis.
insights.quote: That is the ultimate question that we need to be looking at.

themes.theme: Elasticity of demand and the Jevons-paradox caveat

insights.claim: Jevons paradox (cheaper input raises total spend on it) only holds when demand is highly elastic; for inelastic goods like oil or insulin, making them cheap does not multiply consumption, so 'AI makes X cheap therefore we'll spend more on X' is often wrong.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Don't apply Jevons reflexively; the trade only works for elastic-demand sectors (software), making elasticity the screen for which 'AI-cheapens-it' bets actually grow revenue.
insights.quote: this only happens if the demand for something is highly elastic

insights.claim: Under the O-ring/O*NET task model, if AI automates 9 of 10 tasks and the product gets cheaper, sufficiently elastic demand can raise total volume enough to increase hiring of the remaining human task — which is why software-engineer demand has risen, not fallen.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Automation can be net job-creating where demand is elastic; near-term, AI-exposed elastic sectors may see employment grow, not shrink.

themes.theme: No white-collar bloodbath yet — current data

insights.claim: Across the economy, including the most AI-exposed software engineering, there is currently no real evidence of mass AI-driven automation or unemployment — Yale's Budget Lab analysis shows you 'really have to squint to see anything happening.'
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: The 'AI is already destroying jobs' narrative isn't yet in the macro data; fade trades premised on imminent labor collapse until the data turns.
insights.quote: you really have to squint to see anything happening

insights.claim: The only visible signal is junior developer hiring running modestly below trend (slower growth, not a level drop), while demand for senior software engineers has if anything increased.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: AI exposure is hitting the bottom of the skill ladder first; the early labor signal is compressed entry-level hiring, not aggregate displacement.
insights.quote: it's a bit below trend

insights.claim: Reported AI layoffs may be coordination-driven theater: a narrative that not laying people off signals failure to adopt AI can cascade firms into layoffs that leave them worse off, just to be seen as 'using AI.'
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Discount AI-layoff headlines as partly signaling/keeping-up-with-the-Joneses rather than genuine productivity displacement.
insights.quote: the firm might actually be worse off after the layoffs than before

insights.claim: Beyond the O-ring reliability barrier, demand for lawyers, accountants and engineers persists because buyers pay for accountability, ownership, regulatory backing and licensing — frictions that keep humans in the loop independent of capability.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Regulatory/liability moats, not skill, protect many professional jobs near-term — but the speakers view these as 'transitional,' so the moat erodes as institutions adapt.
insights.quote: There's a lot of regulatory layers that are also going to be keeping... humans in the loop

themes.theme: Why the recession / negative-growth scenario is improbable

insights.claim: The viral Citrini scenario (white-collar automation kills salaries, demand slumps, recession) is largely implausible: getting negative economic growth requires capital owners to hit a hard demand bound, refuse to consume more, AND refuse to invest the freed resources — conditions the speakers judge very unlikely.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Fade the AI-driven-recession thesis; in a singularity-adjacent world the natural response is more data-center/fab investment, which sustains growth.
insights.quote: For abundance to generate negative economic growth, that's really hard to get.

insights.claim: Unlike the Great Depression (where the technological frontier did not expand), an AI scenario has an expanding frontier and genuine abundance, so demand-collapse dynamics that shrink the economy are far harder to trigger.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Expanding-frontier abundance is structurally different from a deflationary depression; don't map Depression-style demand-collapse models onto AGI.
insights.quote: Here, the technological frontier is expanding. You actually have abundance.

themes.theme: The 'messy middle' and political economy of displacement

insights.claim: The worst realistic scenario is the 'messy middle' (Molly Kinder): AI automates enough to displace workers but creates too little new wealth to fund a Pareto-improving payoff, with allocative and political frictions blocking targeted redistribution.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The tail risk for markets is not collapse but a politically destabilizing slow grind; watch redistribution-policy fights as the channel for volatility.

insights.claim: Like 1920-1940 phone operators (automated over 20 years via a slow 'drip,' reabsorbed at lower salaries and underemployed per a QJE paper), the danger is not mass unemployment but workers drifting into lower-paying sectors — politically corrosive precisely because it's gradual.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: The displacement signature to watch is falling wages / downgrading, not headline unemployment; wage-mix data may matter more than the unemployment rate.
insights.quote: they got reabsorbed into the economy, but at lower salaries

insights.claim: Politics flips on small moves: per Andy Hall, a 2% rise in unemployment completely changes the political winds, and a fast 2-3% uptick could trigger a COVID-scale emergency fiscal response.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: A 2-3% fast unemployment jump is the political tripwire for emergency stimulus/redistribution — a key macro-policy threshold to monitor.
insights.quote: If there's a 2% increase in unemployment, the political winds completely change.

insights.claim: The messy middle requires several improbable things to all hold (AI that automates entire white-collar jobs only piecemeal, while being only marginally cheaper than humans), and the speakers' model of intelligence implies that whatever can replace all software engineers can also automate accountants/analysts broadly — making the narrow, low-surplus case unlikely.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: If AI is good enough to fully replace one cognitive job it likely generalizes, producing large surplus — so the low-wealth-creation trap is improbable.

insights.claim: Redistribution tools differ in implementation complexity and payoff timing, so the likely outcome is a layered stack — e.g., a negative income tax provides an immediate floor, while universal basic capital won't generate returns for months.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Expect a portfolio of redistribution policies, not one; fast-acting income floors and slow-building capital schemes serve different phases of the transition.

insights.claim: UBI and negative income tax create a dangerous power-sharing arrangement: when people depend on a discretionary government check for basic needs (instead of labor income they own), whoever holds power can revoke it — whereas universal basic capital gives ownership/property rights and shareholder status.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Ownership-based redistribution (capital stakes) is structurally safer than dependency-based transfers; favors policy designs that put equity, not cash entitlements, in citizens' hands.
insights.quote: that feels like a power-sharing arrangement that's really dangerous

insights.claim: Universal basic capital's core flaw is targeting/indexing risk: you can't know which firms will win, so portfolios could be wrecked if, e.g., Anthropic goes to zero while some unknown robotics company captures everything.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The investability of AGI gains depends on being able to index broadly; concentrated, hard-to-predict winners are the central risk to capital-distribution schemes.
insights.quote: what if Anthropic goes to zero, but some random robotics company takes all this over?

insights.claim: Revenue source, tax base, and distribution should be separated: a broad-based tax (consumption/VAT or land) can fund the government buying equity (e.g., Anthropic shares) and distributing it — echoing David Autor's proposal and the privatize-Social-Security idea of handing everyone a basket of stocks.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: The cleanest AGI redistribution is tax-and-buy-equity; watch for VAT/consumption-tax-funded sovereign equity programs as a policy vector that would bid up broad equity ownership.

insights.claim: A wealth/capital tax risks having no stable equilibrium at a low rate — like the income tax it could start small (e.g., 0.5%, for war) and escalate, and could distort investment by diluting shareholders' stakes.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Wealth-tax regimes tend to ratchet up; investors should weight escalation and investment-distortion risk when modeling post-AGI capital taxation.
insights.quote: there's no politically sustainable equilibrium at a 0.5% wealth tax

insights.claim: A Georgist (land) tax would not raise enough revenue for serious AGI redistribution, because land/housing's value comes mainly from proximity to other humans — a factor that won't be the main factor of production in the future.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Land-value taxation is too small a base for AGI-scale redistribution; housing-as-wealth is poorly positioned for the AI economy.

themes.theme: Capital accumulation, satiation, and who ends up owning the future

insights.claim: Most billionaire wealth is compounding stock, not consumption: Zuckerberg keeps Meta building data centers rather than paying himself dividends, revealing an almost 'Nick Landian' preference for accelerating capital — so the wealthiest already behave like non-satiating capital accumulators.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: If the richest reinvest rather than consume, capital share rises endogenously; this favors capital owners and the compounding-reinvestment AI buildout over consumption-driven labor demand.
insights.quote: he would rather have his wealth compound and have Meta build more data centers

insights.claim: If even a few agents don't satiate in capital (driven by exploration, philanthropy, political/religious influence, or pure accumulation), rational behavior gives them the highest savings rate, so in the long run they hold most wealth and the overall capital share converges to their spending pattern — i.e., toward one.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Labor share's fate may be decided by a handful of non-satiating mega-accumulators; the existence of such agents tilts the long run toward capital, not labor.
insights.quote: the overall capital share will basically be the capital share of that person's spending, which is going to be one

insights.claim: Historically, dynastic capital didn't take over the economy because of 'dissipation shocks' — heirs squander wealth or foundations spend it down — but if people live forever or align trusts to perpetual accumulation, those shocks vanish and concentration could compound unchecked.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Longevity and better wealth-perpetuation vehicles remove the historical brake on concentration; watch life-extension and perpetual-trust structures as drivers of extreme capital concentration.
insights.quote: They've given it to their kids who squandered it, or they put it in foundations which spent it.

insights.claim: If society satiates in capital, returns to capital fall, the rich consume more and invest less; conversely sustained high returns (e.g., data centers earning ~30%) signal continued non-satiation — so the returns-to-capital path is the general-equilibrium tell for which scenario we're in.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Track the realized return on AI capital (data centers): persistently high returns mean non-satiation and rising capital share; falling returns flag the satiation/high-labor-share branch.

insights.claim: Investment-specific technical change means the price of capital goods can fall faster than the capital stock grows (one robot becomes many robots while ballerina count is fixed), so standard one-good macro models that treat output as interchangeable between capital and consumption will mislead in this world.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Relative-price dynamics dominate: nominal/real interest rates and capital returns can look wildly different across robot-units vs. consumption-units; standard macro intuition about rates and growth breaks down.
insights.quote: The price of capital is falling relative to the price of consumption

themes.theme: Non-human preferences — AIs, firms, and von Neumann probes

insights.claim: Evolutionary selection among AI-containing firms will favor entities that grow and reproduce, which implies a preference for unsatiable accumulation of the relevant scarce resource (e.g., compute) — and such growth-maximizing agents will not prefer human-intrinsic goods, biasing future production away from the relational sector.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Future demand may be set by growth-selected AI/firm agents that buy compute and capital, not human services — structurally bearish for the human-relational economy's share.
insights.quote: It will probably favor firms or agents that grow.

insights.claim: Whether AI 'beings' show up as raising or lowering labor share is partly an accounting choice: a self-owning von Neumann probe optimizing between making more probes vs. buying a ballerina has high marginal value for resources and near-zero value for human goods, but only final consumption and investment count in GDP.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Post-AGI 'labor share' figures may be artifacts of how AI agents are accounted for; headline macro stats could mislead about who actually captures resources.
insights.quote: it just completely depends on how we're doing the accounting there

insights.claim: Human preference for genuine human connection over AI substitutes may strengthen, not fade, via natural selection: people with a moral aversion (Haidt framework) to offloading social interaction to AI are likelier to find mates and reproduce, selecting for stronger pro-human preference over generations.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: If relational preference is selected-for, the human-intrinsic sector has a durable demand floor — but this is highly speculative and conditional on reproduction patterns holding.

insights.claim: Once AIs run production in 'neuralese' thousands of times faster, transaction costs and reliability concerns will make integrating any human into future production flows hard, even where a human has comparative advantage — so production will be organized around AI labor by default.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Comparative advantage won't save human labor if integration frictions dominate; AI-native production stacks will exclude humans structurally, beyond mere cost.
insights.quote: there will be whole production flows organized for AI labor

themes.theme: Capturing AGI gains — indexing, housing, and developing countries

insights.claim: For both displaced workers and poor countries, buying the index of AGI (broad capital ownership) is a cleaner, more likely-to-succeed strategy than retraining or building local data centers — because labor income, not capital ownership, is what AGI undermines.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Position for AGI via diversified capital ownership rather than skills bets; policy that subsidizes citizen equity ownership beats jobs/retraining programs.
insights.quote: buying the index of AGI... is probably a much cleaner strategy and much more likely to succeed

insights.claim: The average person's capital is mostly a house, which is uniquely ill-suited to complement AI/robot production and won't be bid up by the rich — whereas AI wealth is increasingly in concentrated private companies (OpenAI, Anthropic) ordinary investors can't access.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Home equity is the wrong asset for the AI economy; access to AI-exposed (often private) capital is the real wealth lever, and a structural inequality driver.
insights.quote: Most of their capital is having a random house

insights.claim: The decisive macro question is whether AGI resembles electricity (downstream users capture the benefits, producers like ConEd hold little rent/power) or social media (ubiquitous but rents accrue to the platform) — this determines whether AGI gains can be indexed or are monopolized.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: The electricity-vs-social-media question is the master frame for AI equity strategy: commoditized AGI means buy-the-index works; platform-capture AGI means a few labs capture everything.
insights.quote: Is AI going to be like electricity or social media?

insights.claim: If open models stay only ~6-9 months behind the frontier (plus continual/online learning), then within months of AGI everyone gains access, commoditizing the labs and letting even Nigeria 'just buy the index' — making the recursive-self-improvement/continual-learning timeline a direct input to who captures AGI returns.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: The open-vs-closed-model gap is the swing variable for AI rent capture; track open-model lag and continual-learning progress as signals for lab pricing power.
insights.quote: in six months, everybody has access to this resource

insights.claim: Despite a recent uptick in privatized returns, well under 20% of the total market cap of non-tiny US companies is private, and frictions keeping firms (including OpenAI/Anthropic) private — disclosure requirements etc. — may themselves be reduced by AI, so the long trend toward easier indexing likely continues.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Indexing AI gains is less broken than headlines suggest, and the labs likely go public; the base case favors broad public-equity access to AI value over time.
insights.quote: well under 20% of the total market cap of non-tiny companies in the US is private

insights.claim: Developing countries should prioritize indexing into AI supply chains now (via sovereign wealth funds or citizen subsidies) over retraining, because their education systems are weak and AI could enable leapfrogging — as Nigeria leapfrogged into mobile banking ahead of Germany.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Allocators advising emerging-market states should favor capital-market exposure to AI supply chains (HBM, fabs, ASML-type, labs) over domestic jobs programs.

themes.theme: Commoditization vs. concentration — safety and political risk

insights.claim: AI will more likely produce broad prosperity if its gains are as hard to capture as electrification's; commoditized frontier models diffuse benefits, whereas concentrated labs concentrate surplus narrowly.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Bet on broad-based AI prosperity only in the commoditized branch; concentrated-lab dominance implies a few winners capture the surplus.

insights.claim: Commoditizing frontier AI carries a safety cost — a tech-race dynamic where no single lab has the buffer to slow down — but the speakers argue the trade-off is smaller than assumed because a leader-laggard gap is compatible with the leader being a widely-owned public company rather than fantastically rich private one.
insights.speaker: Phil Trammell
insights.so_what: Safety-driven concentration and broad ownership aren't strictly opposed; widely-held public AI leaders could square distributed gains with a capability buffer.

insights.claim: Concentrated labs create a tangible political target for government expropriation — illustrated by the Defense Production Act threat against Anthropic — a risk that diffuses and is harder to exercise if many labs are at the frontier.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Single-lab dominance is a political/regulatory tail risk (nationalization, DPA-style action); concentration risk is a factor when valuing frontier AI equity.
insights.quote: We saw this with the Defense Production Act threat against Anthropic.

insights.claim: The negative AI narrative dominates not because it's more accurate but because loss of an existing thing is psychologically easier to convey than an as-yet-nonexistent utopia — a framing asymmetry that biases public and political sentiment.
insights.speaker: Alex Imas
insights.so_what: Persistent negative AI sentiment is partly a framing artifact, not a fundamentals signal; useful for gauging when pessimism is overdone in AI-exposed assets.
insights.quote: It's much easier for somebody to go on a podcast and say, 'These jobs that you like, they're going away'
